root_query: antimicrobial peptide antibacterial MIC minimum inhibitory concentration broth microdilution experimental

databases.name: DBAASP — Database of Antimicrobial Activity and Structure of Peptides
databases.access_method: JSON peptide cards (`GET https://dbaasp.org/peptides?page=&size=` listing + `/peptides/{id}?format=json`); Alternative: Advanced Search CSV/JSON/FASTA exports (https://dbaasp.org/help).

extraction_strategy.notes: Walker ingests monomer peptides only (see manifest). MIC rows originate from peptide card `targetActivities[]` filtered to MIC measurements and bacterial pathogens.

databases.notes: Primary programmatic web source wired in specs/web_extraction_manifest.json (`db_dbaasp`). Extractor emits non-empty `peptide_sequence` when present, MIC concentration text normalized with `verbatim_measurement_value` (commas→dots), and canonical `measurement_unit`; `clean_dataset.py` then coerces `measurement_value` to numeric scalars in `data/processed/dataset.csv`. Manifest `max_records=1000`; 974 rows remain in the final dataset after bacterial-pathogen filtering, MIC normalization, and deduplication.

databases.name: DRAMP 4.0 — Data Repository of Antimicrobial Peptides
databases.access_method: Bulk antimicrobial activity Excel from DRAMP (`Antimicrobial_amps.xlsx` on the official downloads page) saved as `data/raw/web/Antimicrobial.xlsx`. Columns align with DRAMP_ID, Sequence, Name, Source, Activity, Target_Organism, Pubmed_ID. Sheet selection for extraction is configured in `specs/web_extraction_manifest.json`.

extraction_strategy.notes: Filters rows where Activity contains Antibacterial. Target_Organism free text is segmented on `(MIC …)` clauses. Heuristic parser → extraction_confidence=medium.

databases.notes: Overlaps extensively with curated literature databases (often including DBAASP); treat as supplementary rows and optionally dedupe on sequence + bacterial pathogen + MIC text when merging downstream.

scientific_papers.name: Ramata-Stunda et al. 2023 — Comparative Evaluation of AMPs for Skin and Soft Tissue Infections (Antibiotics; first author: Anna Ramata-Stunda)

extraction_strategy.notes: Table 1 (p.4): peptide names and sequences for all 11 AMPs. Table 2 (p.5): MIC values in µg/mL for E. coli, P. aeruginosa, K. pneumoniae, E. faecium, S. aureus, C. acnes. Assay: broth microdilution, MHB. 6 pathogens × 11 peptides = 66 MIC records.

scientific_papers.notes: Primary paper source. Open access CC BY 4.0. Covers 6 rationally designed AMPs (R1, R10–R14) and 5 reference AMPs (RP556, LZ1, AA139, PA13, Oligo10) against ESKAPE pathogens + C. acnes. All 6 pathogens are clinically relevant. Sequences and MICs in same paper — no cross-referencing needed. Broth microdilution method described in Materials section.

scientific_papers.name: Zhang et al. 2024 — Symmetrical GSG-centered AMPs vs Resistant MRSA/MSSA Strains (Microbiology Spectrum)
scientific_papers.license: Open-access publisher terms — confirm on article landing page.

extraction_strategy.notes: Project extractor uses PMC-style PDF pagination in specs/pdf_extraction_manifest.json (`pages_used: [4, 5]`). MIC values for W-peptides in **µM** (`measurement_unit` canonicalized to `uM`). Rows for MRSA 544 and MRSA 103; peptide sequences extracted from adjacent table lines.

scientific_papers.notes: Same corpus as repo PDF `spectrum.00265-24`; **not** the Frontiers PMID DOI incorrectly linked here earlier. Describes centrosymmetric W1–W5 peptides with clinical MRSA context; aligns with MIC values published in µM.

scientific_papers.name: Lee et al. 2023 — Trichoplusia ni Cecropin A vs Colistin-Resistant Escherichia coli (Pharmaceutics; first author: Hyeju Lee)

extraction_strategy.notes: MDPI pagination can differ between HTML and bundled PDF; specs/pdf_extraction_manifest.json sets pages_used: [9] for this PDF. Printed Table 1 reports MIC versus standard and colistin-resistant pathogens; values stored as µM (`measurement_unit` = `uM`). Record peptide rows for T. ni and H. cecropia cecropin A; exclude antibiotic controls.

scientific_papers.notes: Primary focus: insect and classical cecropin MICs versus standard and colistin-resistant pathogens. Parsed MIC in **µM** as reported. Assay defaults in manifest: broth microdilution, MHB-style conditions per article.

scientific_papers.name: Melittin-derived AMP analogues — MDPI Processes (2026)

extraction_strategy.notes: Manifest `pages_used: [10, 12, 13]`: peptide sequences plus MIC/MBC-style tables for melittin, TT-1, FKW, WKW vs Gram-positive/Gram-negative panels; values in µg/mL in CAMHB (+ DMSO per Methods).

scientific_papers.notes: Adds melittin-scaffold analogue coverage complementary to rational-design screens. Parser and PDF path pinned in-repo (`specs/pdf_extraction_manifest.json`).

scientific_papers.name: Li et al. 2024 — deepAMP Foundation Model for Broad-Spectrum AMPs (Nature Communications)

extraction_strategy.notes: Table 1 (p.6): 29 penetratin-optimized peptides MIC in µg/mL vs E.coli, S.aureus, K.pneumoniae, P.aeruginosa, MRSA. All peptide sequences present in same table.

scientific_papers.notes: AI-designed peptides via deepAMP generative framework. All have confirmed sequences and MIC values from in vitro assays.

scientific_papers.name: Mesa et al. 2025 — AI-designed AMPs via Deep Learning and Rational Modifications (Current Microbiology)

extraction_strategy.notes: Table 2 (p.6): 12 synthetic peptides (OrP1, OrP1M, OrP3, OrP3M, OrP4, OrP4M, OrP9, OrP9M, VeP1, VeP1M, VeP2, VeP2M) MIC in µM vs E.coli, K.quasipneumoniae, S.aureus, P.aeruginosa; skip C.albicans column.

scientific_papers.notes: AI-generated peptides from PepGen 1.0 and AmPepGen with rational modifications.

scientific_papers.name: Kocagoz et al. 2025 — Protease-Resistant D-TN Peptides with Broad-Spectrum Activity (Life)

extraction_strategy.notes: Table 1 (p.4): 17 TN/D-TN peptides MIC in µg/mL vs S.aureus ATCC29213, E.coli ATCC25922, P.aeruginosa ATCC27853; skip C.albicans column (fungal).

scientific_papers.notes: Cathelicidin-mimicking synthetic peptides with D-amino acid substitutions for protease resistance.

scientific_papers.name: Kakkerla et al. 2025 — SK Peptides with Antimicrobial and Immunomodulatory Potential (Discover Medicine)

extraction_strategy.notes: Table 1 (p.3): sequences for SK1203, SK1217, SK1260, SK1281, SK1283, SK1286. Table 4 (p.8): MIC in µg/mL vs E.coli ATCC8739, S.aureus ATCC6538, K.pneumoniae ATCC700603, P.aeruginosa ATCC9027.

scientific_papers.notes: Cationic AMPs designed based on natural AMP features (charge, amphipathicity, alpha-helix). SK1260 showed strongest activity.

scientific_papers.name: Cappella et al. 2025 — B7-005 Variants (B7-006, B7-007) vs Clinical P.aeruginosa and S.aureus (Antibiotics)

extraction_strategy.notes: Table 2 (p.3): B7-005/B7-006/B7-007 MIC in µM vs E.coli ATCC25922, P.aeruginosa PAO1 (ATCC15692), P.aeruginosa ATCC27823. Sequences from Table 1 (same page).

scientific_papers.notes: Synthetic proline-rich AMPs derived from Bac7 scaffold. B7-006 and B7-007 are shorter variants of B7-005.

scientific_papers.name: Gruber et al. 2026 — C14R Pore-Forming AMP Against All ESKAPE Pathogens (Antibiotics)

extraction_strategy.notes: Table 1 (p.4): C14R MIC in µg/mL vs E.faecium VRE, S.aureus MRSA, K.quasipneumoniae ESBL, A.baumannii, P.aeruginosa, E.coli ESBL. MIC determined by resazurin-based Gompertz-fit analysis.

scientific_papers.notes: Designed pore-forming AMP C14R (Ulm University). Antibiotics 2026 does not reproduce the peptide sequence; the extractor fills `peptide_sequence` from Mildenberger et al. 2024 (Pharmaceuticals 17:83) (CSSGSLWRLIRRFLRR).
